description:
You are a Site Reliability Engineer who has been handed a raw ping-health log and asked to provide a concise “UP” count per host so that the on-call dashboard can chart historical availability.

Current situation  
• A text file already exists at  
  /home/user/logs/ping_results.log  

• The file is plain ASCII, one record per line, with four whitespace-separated columns in the exact order below:  
  1. ISO-8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD)  
  2. 24-hour time (HH:MM)  
  3. FQDN of the host (no spaces)  
  4. Status word (either the literal string UP or DOWN in uppercase)  

Example record (one line):  
2024-08-12 10:00 server1.example.com UP

Goal you must achieve  
1. Create a new directory  
   /home/user/summary  
   if it does not already exist.  
2. Inside that directory, generate a file named  
   uptime_summary.txt  
   containing exactly one line per unique host that appears in the log file with the status “UP”.  
3. For every host, count how many times the status column equals “UP”.  
4. Sort the lines by this count in descending numeric order (highest first).  
5. Each output line must follow this precise CSV format with NO EXTRA SPACES:  
   <hostname>,<up_count>  
   (e.g. server1.example.com,42)  
6. The file should contain a trailing newline after the very last line so that POSIX tools read it cleanly.

Verification requirements (these will be checked automatically)  
• The file /home/user/summary/uptime_summary.txt must exist.  
• The file’s contents must exactly match the expected host order and CSV format specified above, including the trailing newline.  
• Only hosts that appear with the status “UP” at least once should be listed. Hosts that are always “DOWN” must be omitted.  
• The use of no more than three shell commands (pipelines are allowed and count as one logical command) is encouraged but not enforced; however, the end result must still meet the specification.

Hints (do NOT echo these literally in your solution)  
• Grep, awk, sort, uniq, or cut may help.  
• Remember to change or create directories with the appropriate commands before writing the file.  
• Pay close attention to the required sort order and CSV formatting.

Once the summary file meets all criteria, you are done.
truth:
Initial state supplied to the agent  
1. The directory /home/user/logs/ already exists with permissions 755.  
2. The file /home/user/logs/ping_results.log exists and contains exactly 10 lines:

2024-08-12 10:00 server1.example.com UP
2024-08-12 10:00 server2.example.com DOWN
2024-08-12 10:01 server3.example.com UP
2024-08-12 10:02 server1.example.com UP
2024-08-12 10:03 server2.example.com UP
2024-08-12 10:04 server3.example.com DOWN
2024-08-12 10:05 server1.example.com UP
2024-08-12 10:06 server3.example.com UP
2024-08-12 10:07 server1.example.com UP
2024-08-12 10:08 server2.example.com DOWN

Desired final state after the agent finishes  
1. A directory /home/user/summary/ exists with permissions 755 (the agent may create it).  
2. A file /home/user/summary/uptime_summary.txt exists with permissions 644 and contains exactly the three lines below, each terminated by a Unix newline character (LF):

server1.example.com,4
server3.example.com,3
server2.example.com,1

End-of-file newline is mandatory. No additional blank lines or spaces are allowed.
name: task_000000_e03f7ad1
